flags.title: GAAP results turned to a net loss and operating cash outflow nearly doubled in Q1 2026, while management continues to report a large positive 'adjusted' profit built on an expanding, and now understated, inventory step-up addback
flags.description: The annual-filing gap between GAAP and adjusted earnings has now manifested as an outright GAAP net loss even as adjusted net earnings remained solidly positive. For the three months ended March 29, 2026, Gildan reported a GAAP net loss of $65.8 million (continuing operations loss of $55.1 million) versus adjusted net earnings from continuing operations of $80.0 million — a $145.8 million swing driven substantially by the Hanes inventory fair-value step-up ($106.3 million expensed in the quarter alone, with $95 million still outstanding, an amount which had previously been estimated in the FY2025 annual filing at $201.6 million total but is now guided to fully unwind within approximately five months). Simultaneously, cash flow used in operating activities nearly doubled to $279.4 million from $142.2 million in the prior-year quarter, evidencing that the divergence between reported earnings quality and cash generation identified in the prior annual filing is intensifying rather than normalizing post-acquisition, at the same time management is asking investors to look through GAAP losses via an even larger set of non-GAAP addbacks.

quotes.location: Exhibit 99.2 (Q1 2026), Condensed Interim Consolidated Statements of Earnings

quotes.text: Cash flows from (used in) operating activities | (279,474) | (142,233)
quotes.location: Exhibit 99.2 (Q1 2026), Condensed Interim Consolidated Statements of Cash Flows

quotes.text: Adjusted net earnings from continuing operations(2) | 80.0 | 89.9
quotes.location: Exhibit 99.1 (Q1 2026 MD&A), Section 5.5 "Net earnings, adjusted net earnings, and earnings per share measures"

quotes.text: Inventory fair value step-up cost recorded as part of the Hanes business acquisition(1) | 106.3 | — ... The residual step up cost, of $95 million, is expected to turn over within approximately five months.
quotes.location: Exhibit 99.1 (Q1 2026 MD&A), Section 5.4.2 and Section 15.0 (non-GAAP definitions)

flags.verification_path: Track the Q2/Q3 fiscal 2026 filings to confirm the $95 million residual inventory step-up is fully expensed as guided and does not recur or get replaced by new one-time addbacks; reconcile cumulative GAAP net earnings/loss versus cumulative adjusted net earnings and versus cumulative operating cash flow over fiscal 2026 to determine whether the gap narrows as integration normalizes; compare the magnitude and frequency of new non-GAAP adjustment categories introduced each quarter to assess whether the non-GAAP framework is being used to smooth genuinely volatile acquisition-related costs versus obscuring deteriorating core profitability; examine subsequent-quarter working capital cash usage to see if the $254.5 million negative working-capital swing in Q1 2026 (largely a $211.5 million paydown of accounts payable) recurs.
flags.additional_data_that_would_change_confid: Q2 and Q3 fiscal 2026 results confirming the step-up fully unwinds as guided, along with a return to GAAP profitability and positive operating cash flow, would increase confidence this is a transitory acquisition effect; continued GAAP losses or cash burn beyond the guided period, or introduction of further new addback categories, would confirm the divergence is structural rather than transitory.

flags.title: Newly disclosed exclusion of Hanes (approximately 45% of net sales and a majority of the combined balance sheet) from the scope of ICFR and disclosure controls evaluation creates a significant control-assurance gap during the critical post-acquisition integration period
flags.description: The Q1 2026 interim filing discloses for the first time in the documents reviewed that management has explicitly excluded Hanes' internal control over financial reporting (ICFR) and disclosure controls and procedures (DC&P) from its evaluation scope, even though Hanes-derived net sales represented approximately 45% of total net sales in the quarter and Hanes contributed billions of dollars to the consolidated balance sheet ($2.3 billion of current assets, $3.8 billion of non-current assets, $1.0 billion of current liabilities, and $1.7 billion of non-current liabilities). This means that nearly half of consolidated revenue and the majority of consolidated assets are being reported without the auditor's or management's ICFR/DC&P assurance framework having been extended to them, at precisely the time restructuring, facility closures, integration adjustments, and purchase-accounting entries (all judgment-intensive) are being pushed through the combined company's books.

quotes.text: As disclosed in our 2025 Annual MD&A, we have limited the scope of our evaluation of internal controls over financial reporting (ICFR) to exclude controls over financial reporting of Hanes, a subsidiary acquired by the Company in connection with an acquisition consummated on December 1, 2025. In addition, we have also limited the scope of our evaluation of disclosure controls and procedures (DC&P) to exclude disclosure controls and procedures of Hanes.
quotes.location: Exhibit 99.1 (Q1 2026 MD&A), Section 13.0 "Internal Control Over Financial Reporting"

quotes.text: The net sales originating from the financial records of Hanes which were included in the condensed interim consolidated results of the Company for the three month period ended March 29, 2026 represented approximately 45% of total net sales. Hanes accounted for approximately $2,313 million of current assets, $3,809 million of non-current assets, $986 million of current liabilities, and $1,703 million of non-current liabilities, in the Company's condensed interim consolidated statement of financial position as at March 29, 2026.
quotes.location: Exhibit 99.1 (Q1 2026 MD&A), Section 13.0 "Internal Control Over Financial Reporting"

quotes.text: Restructuring and acquisition-related costs for the three months ended March 29, 2026 primarily reflect expenses associated with the integration of Hanes. These costs include $27.1 million for severance and other related charges...and $14.5 million for the write-off of equipment related to these facility closures.

flags.verification_path: Confirm the standard SEC/SOX transition period exclusion for recently acquired businesses (typically permissible for up to one year) and check whether Gildan discloses a specific date by which Hanes ICFR will be brought into scope; review the FY2026 annual filing (expected early 2027) to see whether management's assessment of ICFR effectiveness includes or continues to exclude Hanes, and whether any material weaknesses are identified once Hanes is in scope; compare disclosed restructuring/integration costs and PPA measurement-period adjustments occurring during the exclusion window for any subsequent restatement or correction that might indicate the excluded controls were in fact deficient; review the external auditor's (KPMG) report for any scope limitation language regarding ICFR.
flags.additional_data_that_would_change_confid: The specific timeline management commits to for bringing Hanes into ICFR scope, and the eventual FY2026 ICFR assessment outcome (including any material weaknesses identified), would directly confirm or dismiss the severity of this gap; SEC guidance/no-action correspondence on the length of permissible exclusion would also inform whether this is standard practice or an extended, atypical scope limitation.

flags.title: Net debt leverage ratio disclosure inconsistency has widened rather than narrowed: reported non-GAAP leverage of 3.3x now diverges by a full 0.5 turn from the leverage ratio used under U.S. private placement note covenants (3.8x)
flags.description: The prior-period flag regarding multiple, inconsistent leverage metrics has been confirmed and has intensified in Q1 2026. The Company's headline non-GAAP 'net debt leverage ratio' increased to 3.3x (from 3.0x at fiscal year-end), still calculated using a pro-forma adjusted EBITDA that includes $472.0 million of Hanes/HAA EBITDA for periods substantially before Gildan owned the business. However, the leverage ratio actually used for its term loan and revolving facility covenants was 3.4x, and for its U.S. private placement note covenants was 3.8x — up from 3.1x and 3.4x, respectively, at fiscal year-end. The gap between the headline non-GAAP figure and the most conservative covenant-basis figure widened from 0.4x to 0.5x in a single quarter, and the pro-forma EBITDA add-on still represents roughly 32% of the pro-forma adjusted EBITDA figure used in the leverage calculation, meaning a material share of the leverage calculation still rests on unaudited, Hanes-supplied historical figures rather than actual trailing results under Gildan ownership.

quotes.text: Gildan's net debt leverage ratio as at March 29, 2026 was 3.3 times (3.0 times at December 28, 2025).
quotes.location: Exhibit 99.1 (Q1 2026 MD&A), Section 8.2 "Long-term debt and net debt and net debt leverage ratio"

quotes.text: The Company's net debt to EBITDA ratio for purposes of its term loans and revolving facility was 3.4x and for purposes of U.S. private placement notes was 3.8x at March 29, 2026 (3.1x and 3.4x respectively at December 28, 2025).

Adjusted EBITDA for the trailing twelve months (excluding discontinued operations) (1) | 981.3 | 926.3 ... Business acquisitions(3) | 472.0 | 564.8 ... Pro-forma adjusted EBITDA for the trailing twelve months | 1,453.3 | 1,491.1
quotes.location: Exhibit 99.1 (Q1 2026 MD&A), Section 8.2, Net debt leverage ratio table

quotes.text: As a result of the closing of the Hanes acquisition, our net debt leverage ratio exceeded our stated target range. Accordingly, we paused our share repurchases starting in August 2025, and we expect share repurchases to resume when the net debt leverage ratio approximates the midpoint of the target range.

flags.verification_path: Obtain the actual credit agreement and note purchase agreement covenant maximums to determine current headroom under the 3.4x and 3.8x figures; recompute an 'as-reported-only' leverage ratio using only actual trailing-twelve-month adjusted EBITDA ($981.3 million) against net debt ($4,867.6 million) to derive an approximate 5.0x ratio excluding all pro-forma adjustments, and compare this to the 3.3x headline figure; monitor the pro-forma add-on ($472.0 million) shrinking toward zero over the next two to three quarters as actual Hanes-inclusive trailing EBITDA replaces the estimate, which will reveal whether underlying Hanes profitability supports the assumed pro-forma figure; confirm continued covenant compliance disclosures each quarter for signs of tightening headroom.
flags.additional_data_that_would_change_confid: The specific maximum leverage covenant thresholds and calculated headroom under each facility, and the trend of the pro-forma EBITDA adjustment shrinking to zero by Q4 2026 (when trailing-twelve-month figures will fully reflect actual post-acquisition Hanes performance) would clarify whether the diverging ratios represent a temporary reporting artifact or a genuine, worsening leverage/covenant risk.

flags.title: Receivables-factoring balance swung sharply downward quarter-over-quarter, and management explicitly attributes part of the accounts-receivable increase and cash-flow weakness to reduced use of this off-balance-sheet financing lever, confirming its outsized influence on reported working capital and operating cash flow
flags.description: Consistent with the prior flag on off-balance-sheet receivables factoring, Q1 2026 data confirms the program's material and volatile impact on reported metrics — but in the opposite direction this quarter, underscoring how discretionary changes in factoring volume can swing reported cash flow and receivables balances. The balance of receivables serviced but sold off-balance-sheet fell from $777.0 million to $667.3 million quarter-over-quarter (a 14% decline), and management explicitly cites 'lower sales of trade accounts receivables to financial institutions under receivables purchase agreements' as a driver of the on-balance-sheet AR increase, alongside 'higher days sales outstanding (DSO) as a result of longer payment terms.' Correspondingly, the related supply-chain timing-difference payable (representing amounts owed to the bank counterparty for a one-week remittance lag) fell from $120.1 million to $39.9 million, contributing to a $211.5 million net decrease in accounts payable and accrued liabilities that was a primary driver of the $254.5 million negative working-capital swing and the near-doubling of cash used in operating activities in the quarter.

quotes.text: As at March 29, 2026, trade accounts receivable being serviced under receivables purchase agreements amounted to $667.3 million (December 28, 2025 - $777.0 million).

The increase in trade accounts receivable (which are net of accrued sales discounts) was mainly due to the impact of higher sales in the first quarter of fiscal 2026 compared to the fourth quarter of fiscal 2025, lower sales of trade accounts receivables to financial institutions under receivables purchase agreements, seasonally lower offset for accruals for sales discounts...and partially offset by higher days sales outstanding (DSO) as a result of longer payment terms.
quotes.location: Exhibit 99.1 (Q1 2026 MD&A), Section 6.1 "Current assets and current liabilities"

quotes.text: Accounts payable and accrued liabilities also include balances payable of $39.9 million (December 28, 2025 - $120.1) resulting mainly from a one-week timing difference between the collection of sold receivables and the weekly remittance to the bank counterparty under the receivables purchase agreement

quotes.location: Exhibit 99.2 (Q1 2026), Note 14(c) "Changes in working capital balances"

flags.verification_path: Track the factoring balance and the associated timing-difference payable over the next several quarters to determine whether the Q1 2026 decline is a one-off (e.g., seasonal or a deliberate deleveraging step ahead of the June 16, 2026 expiry of the largest $975 million receivables purchase agreement) or the start of a sustained wind-down; compare quarterly changes in the factoring balance against quarterly changes in reported operating cash flow to isolate how much of the cash flow volatility is attributable to factoring decisions versus organic collections; monitor the June 2026 renewal/expiry of the primary receivables purchase agreement for any changes in terms, capacity, or non-renewal that could further affect reported cash flow and receivables optics.
flags.additional_data_that_would_change_confid: Confirmation of the reason for the Q1 2026 factoring reduction (e.g., program renewal negotiations, counterparty capacity constraints, or a deliberate treasury decision), and the trend in subsequent quarters as the June 2026 agreement expiry approaches, would clarify whether this represents normal program management or an early warning sign of reduced access to this financing source.

flags.title: Extreme, and now independently corroborated, customer concentration risk persists at the whole-company level, with the largest single customer accounting for roughly a third of total sales and the top ten accounting for nearly three-quarters
flags.description: The Annual Information Form corroborates and updates the prior flag on customer concentration using a full-year, whole-company (not just AR) metric: in fiscal 2025, Gildan's largest customer accounted for 33.2% of total sales and its top ten customers accounted for 72.5% of total sales — an extraordinarily concentrated base for a company of Gildan's scale, further magnified by the still-fresh 2024 merger of the two largest wholesale distributors into a single combined entity. This concentration exists against a backdrop where 'our contracts with our customers do not require them to purchase a minimum quantity of our products,' meaning revenue visibility for a third of total sales rests on an at-will purchasing relationship with one counterparty, and the Q1 2026 MD&A separately discloses an 11.9% year-over-year decline in Wholesale channel net sales attributed partly to 'proactive inventory reduction across customer channels,' consistent with a large customer adjusting its own inventory position.

quotes.text: Our total customer base is composed of a relatively small number of significant customers. In fiscal 2025, our largest customer accounted for 33.2% of our total sales, and our top ten customers accounted for 72.5% of our total sales.
quotes.location: Form 40-F Exhibit 99.3 (Annual Information Form), "Description of the Business" - Brands, Products, and Customers

quotes.text: Although we have long term ongoing relationships with many of our customers, our contracts with our customers do not require them to purchase a minimum quantity of our products.
quotes.location: Form 40-F Exhibit 99.3 (Annual Information Form), "Description of the Business" - Brands, Products, and Customers

quotes.text: On October 1, 2024, the Company's two largest wholesale distributor customers, to which Gildan is a significant supplier, closed a transaction combining their businesses. The combination of these two distributors increased our customer sales concentration with the combined entity to approximately 39% of our fiscal 2024 net sales.
quotes.location: Form 40-F Exhibit 99.3 (Annual Information Form), "General Development of the Business" - Industry Landscape

quotes.text: Wholesale sales were $552 million compared to $626 million, down 11.9% versus the prior year due to the aforementioned proactive inventory reduction across our combined customer channels

flags.verification_path: Cross-reference the AIF's 33.2%/72.5% total-sales concentration figures against the fiscal 2025 annual financial statements' Note 28 receivables-based concentration figures (67% of AR, 33% of net sales) to confirm consistency of the underlying customer identity and reconcile any definitional differences (total sales vs. wholesale-only vs. AR-based); obtain the identity and standalone credit profile of the largest distributor (formed via the 2024 merger) through public/credit-rating sources; monitor subsequent-quarter Wholesale channel net sales trends to distinguish temporary destocking from a structural decline in the relationship; review any risk-factor updates in future filings regarding customer concentration or credit terms extended to this customer.
flags.additional_data_that_would_change_confid: Confirmation of the specific customer's identity and independent financial health data, any public news of the distributor's own liquidity or leverage position, and subsequent-quarter data showing whether the 11.9% Wholesale sales decline reflects temporary destocking or an early signal of demand or credit deterioration at the concentrated customer would materially change confidence in the severity of this risk.
